learning_objectives: Analyze principles of security governance frameworks
Apply standards for security governance frameworks
Evaluate challenges in security governance frameworks
Design solutions for security governance frameworks
readings: Primary text on Security Governance Frameworks
NIST/ISO guidance on Security Governance Frameworks
Case studies on Security Governance Frameworks

lecture_notes.introduction: Security governance ensures that cybersecurity decisions are made at the right organizational level, aligned with business strategy, and supported with appropriate resources and accountability. Without effective governance, security becomes a technical function disconnected from business reality, resulting in either excessive spending on low-risk areas or critical under-investment in genuine threats. The modern governance landscape offers multiple frameworks, each with distinct strengths and applicability. NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0, released in February 2024, introduces the Govern function as the foundational element that shapes all other security activities, explicitly recognizing that governance drives the effectiveness of identification, protection, detection, response, and recovery efforts. ISO 27001:2022 provides a certifiable Information Security Management System standard with a risk-based approach and 93 controls organized across four themes. COBIT 2019 takes an enterprise IT governance perspective, defining governance and management objectives that connect security to broader business value delivery. The CISO's organizational placement and reporting structure directly influence security program effectiveness and resource allocation authority.
key_concepts: NIST CSF 2.0: six core functions (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) with Govern as the foundational function establishing organizational context, risk strategy, roles, policy, oversight, and supply chain risk management

ISO 27001:2022 ISMS: Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle with 93 controls in 4 themes (organizational, people, physical, technological) replacing the previous 14-domain structure for more practical implementation
COBIT 2019: governance system with 40 objectives across five domains enabling enterprise-wide alignment of security with business objectives and IT service delivery requirements

CISO reporting structure: CEO reporting provides strategic influence, CIO reporting creates conflicts between delivery and security, board audit committee dotted line provides governance oversight and independence

Security steering committee: cross-functional body including business leaders, IT, legal, risk, and compliance providing strategic direction, resource allocation, and accountability for security decisions
Enterprise risk management integration: cybersecurity risk alongside operational, financial, strategic, and compliance risks with unified appetite framework and consistent assessment methodology
Maturity assessment: NIST CSF implementation tiers (Partial, Risk-Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive) and CMMI-based levels providing roadmaps and peer benchmarking capability

Board-level oversight: SEC disclosure requirements, director fiduciary duties for cyber risk, recommended committee structures, and the increasing expectation of cybersecurity expertise on corporate boards
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping an organization's security program to NIST CSF 2.0, assessing current maturity for each function, identifying gaps against target profile, and developing prioritized improvement roadmap

review.value: The new Govern function elevates governance from implicit assumption to explicit requirement, establishing organizational context, risk strategy, roles, policy, oversight, and supply chain risk as foundations for all security.

Demonstrates systematic security approach but validates the management system, not perfect operation. Continuous improvement between audits is essential for actual security beyond the certification snapshot.
CISOs reporting to CEO achieve better outcomes: larger budgets, faster incident response, stronger culture. CIOs create inherent conflicts between delivery speed and security requirements.
Board defines acceptable aggregate cyber risk. Without it, every risk appears critical and every investment justified. Appetite enables rational prioritization based on organizational tolerance.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of security policy development and lifecycle
Apply standards for security policy development and lifecycle
Evaluate challenges in security policy development and lifecycle
Design solutions for security policy development and lifecycle
readings: Primary text on Security Policy Development and Lifecycle
NIST/ISO guidance on Security Policy Development and Lifecycle
Case studies on Security Policy Development and Lifecycle

lecture_notes.introduction: Policies are the written foundation of a security program, translating governance decisions and regulatory requirements into organizational expectations for all employees, contractors, and systems. A well-designed policy hierarchy establishes what the organization requires through policies approved at the executive level, how those requirements must be met through specific technical standards, what best practices are recommended through guidelines, and step-by-step implementation through procedures. Effective policies are concise enough to be read, specific enough to be actionable, flexible enough to accommodate business evolution, and enforceable through technical controls and management accountability. The lifecycle extends beyond drafting to include stakeholder review, legal alignment, executive approval, version-controlled distribution, awareness training, enforcement monitoring, and periodic review. A mature program includes formal exception processes allowing deviations when justified with compensating controls, management approval, and defined expiration dates. Policy writing follows principles of clarity, measurability, achievability, regulatory alignment, and enforcement support.
key_concepts: Policy hierarchy: policies (executive-approved mandatory requirements), standards (specific technical specifications), guidelines (recommended practices), procedures (step-by-step instructions) creating a layered documentation framework

Essential domains: acceptable use, access control, data classification, incident response, business continuity, change management, vendor security, encryption, remote access, mobile device, and social media policies

Lifecycle: need identification, SME drafting, stakeholder review, legal review, executive approval, version-controlled distribution, training, enforcement monitoring, annual review with trigger-based updates

Writing principles: clear unambiguous language, measurable verifiable requirements, achievable within resources, regulatory alignment, enforcement mechanism support, and audience-appropriate complexity levels

Exception management: formal request with business justification, risk assessment, compensating controls, management approval at appropriate level, defined expiration, and centralized tracking registry

Compliance measurement: automated technical enforcement via DLP and access controls, regular scanning, non-compliance tracking with escalation, trend analysis for systemic issues, and governance reporting
Regulatory integration: mapping policy statements to specific requirements (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR, SOX) ensuring complete coverage with documented traceability from regulation to policy to control
Change management: trigger-based review for regulatory changes and incidents, calendar-based annual review, version control, change documentation, and stakeholder communication for all updates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drafting a data classification policy with four tiers, writing corresponding standards for each level, and designing the exception management process workflow

review.value: Policy: 'Sensitive data must be encrypted at rest.' Standard: 'AES-256-GCM with HSM-managed keys.' Policy states what; standard states how. Separation allows stable policies while standards evolve with technology.

Business sometimes conflicts with policy. Formal exceptions document risk, require approval, define compensating controls, and set expiration dates. Without the process, violations happen silently or security blocks legitimate activity.

Policies without enforcement are suggestions. Combine technical controls (DLP), automated monitoring (compliance scanning), and management accountability (performance reviews) for effective enforcement.
Unreviewed policies become outdated and lose credibility. Annual review assesses regulatory currency, incident-revealed gaps, business changes, and standard technical currency.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of compliance program management
Apply standards for compliance program management
Evaluate challenges in compliance program management
Design solutions for compliance program management
readings: Primary text on Compliance Program Management
NIST/ISO guidance on Compliance Program Management
Case studies on Compliance Program Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations face overlapping compliance requirements from multiple regulations, standards, and contractual obligations simultaneously. Without a unified approach, each obligation is addressed independently, resulting in redundant controls, duplicated audit effort, inconsistent documentation, and compliance fatigue among operational teams responsible for maintaining evidence across multiple frameworks. An effective compliance program maps all requirements to a unified control framework, implements each control once to satisfy multiple obligations, and maintains continuous evidence of control effectiveness through automated monitoring rather than periodic manual assessment. The shift from point-in-time compliance to continuous monitoring represents a fundamental improvement in both security posture and operational efficiency. Traditional annual audits assess control effectiveness at a single point in time, providing no assurance about the other 364 days. Continuous monitoring tools automatically validate control states: encryption status, MFA enforcement, patch compliance, and access review completion. GRC platforms provide centralized management of the compliance lifecycle from requirement tracking through control mapping, evidence collection, testing, remediation, and reporting.
key_concepts: Unified framework: primary framework selection (NIST CSF or ISO 27001), mapping all regulatory requirements to controls, identifying where single implementations satisfy multiple requirements, documenting framework-specific additions

GRC platforms: centralized control library with regulatory mapping, automated evidence collection, workflow management for testing and remediation, real-time compliance dashboards, and audit management portals

Continuous monitoring: automated scanning for encryption, patch compliance, access configuration, and security tool deployment replacing annual assessments with real-time dashboards and failure alerting

Evidence management: automated pipelines from source systems, chain of custody maintenance, organization by control and period, integrity assurance through access controls, and archival for retention requirements
Audit preparation: readiness assessment 90 days before, evidence pre-staging by domain, designated liaisons, interaction protocols, and efficient information request response
Finding remediation: root cause analysis, plan development with timelines and owners, management approval, verification testing, and preventive measures to avoid recurrence across similar controls
SOC 2 Type II: five trust service criteria, Type I vs Type II distinction, observation period selection, complementary user entity controls, and report evaluation for vendor assurance

Testing methodology: first-line self-assessment, second-line independent testing, third-line external audit, sampling for large populations, and automated testing replacing manual procedures where feasible
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating unified control matrix mapping NIST CSF, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, and SOC 2 to single control set, demonstrating how one MFA implementation satisfies all four frameworks

review.value: One control satisfies multiple regulations: AES-256 encryption satisfies PCI-DSS 3.4, HIPAA technical safeguard, ISO 27001 A.8.24, NIST CSF PR.DS-1, and SOC 2 CC6.1. Implement once, test once.
Point-in-time audits snapshot one day. Continuous monitoring provides 365 days of evidence. A day-45 misconfiguration is detected immediately rather than discovered at the day-365 annual audit.
Key criteria: regulatory content breadth, tool integration, workflow automation, reporting flexibility, and total cost. The platform should reduce compliance effort, not add a new system to manage.
Organizations maintaining continuous evidence need minimal preparation. Those scrambling before audits demonstrate event-based rather than continuous compliance, which auditors view negatively.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of risk management integration and quantification
Apply standards for risk management integration and quantification
Evaluate challenges in risk management integration and quantification
Design solutions for risk management integration and quantification
readings: Primary text on Risk Management Integration and Quantification
NIST/ISO guidance on Risk Management Integration and Quantification
Case studies on Risk Management Integration and Quantification

lecture_notes.introduction: Cybersecurity risk management has historically relied on qualitative assessments using subjective High/Medium/Low ratings that fail to support precise business decisions. When the CISO presents a red-yellow-green dashboard, it communicates risks exist but provides no quantitative basis for investment decisions. FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) translates cyber risk into financial terms by decomposing risk into Loss Event Frequency and Loss Magnitude, each further decomposed into measurable components. Using Monte Carlo simulation with probability distributions rather than single-point estimates produces probabilistic financial exposure ranges that enable rational cost-benefit analysis. Integrating cyber risk with enterprise risk management ensures cybersecurity is evaluated alongside operational, financial, strategic, and compliance risks, enabling holistic resource allocation across the organization. Risk treatment decisions follow logically: mitigate where control cost is less than risk reduction, transfer through insurance, accept within appetite, and avoid unacceptable activities.
key_concepts: FAIR methodology: Loss Event Frequency (Threat Event Frequency x Vulnerability) and Loss Magnitude (Primary Loss + Secondary Loss) with Monte Carlo simulation producing probabilistic ranges

Quantitative vs qualitative: qualitative gives direction (H/M/L), quantitative gives financial estimates ($2.4M exposure) enabling cost-benefit analysis for specific control investments at board level
ERM integration: cybersecurity alongside operational, financial, strategic, and compliance risks with unified appetite framework, consistent methodology, and integrated board reporting
Risk register: scenario descriptions, asset valuation, threat characterization, vulnerability assessment, loss estimation, current controls, residual risk, and treatment decisions in financial terms

Treatment strategies: mitigation (controls reducing frequency/magnitude), transfer (insurance for residual risk), acceptance (documented within appetite), avoidance (discontinuing unacceptable activities)
Cyber insurance: quantitative analysis determining coverage levels, policy evaluation against scenarios, understanding exclusions, and demonstrating maturity to underwriters for favorable premiums
Risk appetite: board-level statement defining acceptable aggregate exposure, tolerance thresholds per scenario, escalation when exceeded, and regular review as conditions change

Risk communication: translating technical risk into business language with financial terms and impact scenarios, providing actionable investment recommendations with quantified risk reduction projections
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting FAIR analysis for ransomware: estimating frequency from industry data, assessing vulnerability, estimating loss magnitudes, running Monte Carlo simulation, and comparing against control investment costs

review.value: Translates 'high ransomware risk' into '$3.2M-$8.7M exposure (90% confidence), reducible to $400K-$1.2M with $500K investment.' Enables rational board-level investment based on quantified risk reduction.
Without appetite, every risk is critical and every investment justified. Appetite provides the threshold for prioritization, preventing both over-spending and dangerous under-investment.
Cyber insurance transfers financial impact above retention. Limits should align with quantitative analysis: if worst case costs $10M and organization accepts $1M, insurance covers the $9M gap.
Single-point estimates create false precision. Monte Carlo with distributions produces ranges with confidence: '90% confident loss between $1M and $5M' is more useful for decisions than '$3M.'

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of security awareness and culture
Apply standards for security awareness and culture
Evaluate challenges in security awareness and culture
Design solutions for security awareness and culture
readings: Primary text on Security Awareness and Culture
NIST/ISO guidance on Security Awareness and Culture
Case studies on Security Awareness and Culture

lecture_notes.introduction: Over 80% of breaches involve a human element. Awareness programs must transform employees from weakest link into active defense. Annual compliance training (one-hour video with quiz) fails: knowledge retention drops 90% within 30 days. Effective awareness requires continuous microlearning delivered weekly, just-in-time training at the moment of risk, phishing simulations with progressive difficulty, role-based training targeting specific threats per audience, and positive reinforcement recognizing secure behavior. Measurement must shift from knowledge metrics (quiz scores) to behavioral metrics (click rates, report rates, time-to-report). A strong security culture means employees make secure choices by default because security is embedded in organizational values, not imposed by the security team. Building culture requires executive sponsorship, security champion programs extending reach into every department, and consistent reinforcement that makes security everyone's daily responsibility rather than an annual compliance checkbox exercise.
key_concepts: Program design: audience segmentation (executives, developers, staff, admins), content using real-world examples, delivery via microlearning, gamification, simulations, and just-in-time training at risk points

Phishing simulation: baseline without notification, progressive difficulty (generic to spear-phishing), immediate landing page training for clickers, targeted follow-up for repeat offenders, ethical guidelines preventing shaming

Role-based training: executives need BEC/whale phishing with wire transfer verification, developers need secure coding, admins need privilege management and social engineering, staff need phishing recognition and data handling
Behavioral metrics: click rate trend, report rate (primary active defense indicator), time-to-report, repeat offender rate, near-miss reporting volume, and policy compliance observed behaviors
Culture assessment: validated survey measuring awareness, behavioral intentions, knowledge, compliance attitudes, and organizational climate, benchmarked against industry for meaningful comparison
ROI calculation: incidents avoided x average cost minus program cost, plus help desk ticket reduction, insurance premium savings, and regulatory training compliance value
Gamification: department leaderboards, security awareness events, CTF competitions for technical staff, and rewards programs reinforcing positive security behaviors through recognition

Executive sponsorship: leadership modeling behavior (using MFA, reporting suspicious emails), visible resource commitment, security messaging in communications, and department-level accountability metrics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing three-phase phishing simulation with increasing difficulty, analyzing results to identify vulnerable groups, creating targeted remediation, and building ROI metrics dashboard

review.value: Quiz scores measure knowledge, not behavior. Report rate is the key metric: 70%+ means the organization detects phishing before success. Measure what people do, not what they know.
Executives face targeted BEC with millions at stake. Training must include real executive-targeted examples and verification procedures specific to their authority level.
Never shame employees who click. Treat as learning opportunities. Track improvement. Punishing clicks discourages the reporting behavior that is more valuable than perfect avoidance.
3-5 minute weekly modules produce higher retention than annual hour-long training. Spacing effect builds lasting habits that transfer to real threat encounters.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of security metrics and board-level reporting
Apply standards for security metrics and board-level reporting
Evaluate challenges in security metrics and board-level reporting
Design solutions for security metrics and board-level reporting
readings: Primary text on Security Metrics and Board-Level Reporting
NIST/ISO guidance on Security Metrics and Board-Level Reporting
Case studies on Security Metrics and Board-Level Reporting

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective metrics bridge technical operations and business decisions. Most teams measure what is easy to count rather than what matters. Blocked connections and malware detections are activity metrics showing busyness, not security effectiveness. Meaningful metrics must be actionable (drives decisions), measurable (objective data), relevant (connected to business), and timely (available when needed). Different audiences need different metrics: SOC needs MTTD/MTTR, management needs remediation velocity, boards need annualized risk exposure in dollars. The CISO's critical skill is translating technical reality into business language enabling informed decisions by people who do not understand technical details. Board communication requires financial framing: not '200 unpatched vulnerabilities' but '$2.4M annualized exposure reducible to $200K with $150K investment delivering 10.7x ROI.' Leading indicators (patch compliance, training completion) predict future performance while lagging indicators (incidents, breaches) report past performance; both are essential for governance.
key_concepts: Operational: MTTD, MTTR, MTTC, alert volume/triage rate, false positive ratio, analyst workload, detection coverage against MITRE ATT&CK technique matrix

Tactical: vulnerability remediation velocity by severity, patch compliance against SLA, security tool coverage, phishing results, training completion, access review completion, exception volume and aging
Strategic: annualized risk exposure (FAIR), program maturity score, regulatory compliance status, security investment ROI, peer benchmarking comparisons across all measurable domains

Board reporting: risk posture in financial terms with trends, incident summary with business impact, compliance status with penalty exposure, and investment recommendations with quantified cost-benefit
Dashboard design: audience-appropriate visualization, trend lines, benchmark context, drill-down capability, and objectively defined RAG thresholds rather than subjective color assignments
Benchmarking: NIST CSF tiers or CMMI for peer comparison, identifying gaps relative to industry, and creating maturity roadmaps with measurable milestones and resource requirements
Anti-patterns: vanity metrics (total threats blocked), activity metrics (scans run), contextless metrics (vulnerability count without severity/exposure/trend), and metrics that cannot drive action
Balanced scorecard: leading indicators (training, patches predicting future) combined with lagging indicators (incidents, detection time measuring past) for comprehensive posture assessment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building board dashboard from operational data: translating vulnerability scans, IR metrics, and compliance data into executive visualizations with investment recommendations

review.value: Translate technical to financial: '$2.4M exposure from unpatched systems, reducible to $200K with $150K initiative' delivers 10.7x ROI. Boards act on financial arguments.
MTTD measures undetected attacker dwell time (industry median: 204 days). Reducing MTTD directly reduces breach impact and cost. Most critical operational metric.
Provides improvement roadmap and peer benchmarking. Level 1 to 3 progress indicates documented, repeatable, consistent processes rather than ad hoc individual heroics.
Lagging shows what happened. Leading predicts what will happen. Both essential: lagging validates, leading enables proactive governance decisions.

class_schedule.topic: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Governance
learning_objectives: Analyze principles of business continuity and disaster recovery governance
Apply standards for business continuity and disaster recovery governance
Evaluate challenges in business continuity and disaster recovery governance
Design solutions for business continuity and disaster recovery governance
readings: Primary text on Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Governance
NIST/ISO guidance on Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Governance
Case studies on Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Governance

lecture_notes.introduction: BC/DR ensures survival when controls fail. Ransomware targeting backups has made BC/DR urgently important: traditional assumptions that backups are available may be invalid. Effective programs begin with Business Impact Analysis identifying critical processes, maximum tolerable downtime, dependencies, and financial impact. BIA drives RPO/RTO targets determining recovery strategy: a 4-hour RTO system cannot use a 24-hour cold site. The DR strategy spectrum spans hot sites (minutes RTO), warm sites (hours), cold sites (days/weeks), and cloud-based DR with configurable objectives. Testing progresses from quarterly tabletop exercises through semi-annual walkthroughs, annual simulations, to biennial full interruption tests. Each level validates different aspects: tabletop tests decisions, walkthrough tests procedures, simulation tests coordination, full interruption tests actual recovery. Ransomware-resilient backups require immutable WORM storage, air-gapped copies, regular restore verification, and key management separate from production systems. Integration with incident response ensures coordinated activation when security incidents cause business disruption.
key_concepts: BIA: identifying critical processes, MTPD per process, dependency mapping (technology, people, facilities, vendors), financial impact quantification, and recovery sequence prioritization
RPO/RTO: Recovery Point Objective (max data loss) and Recovery Time Objective (max downtime) per function, driven by BIA, determining architecture and investment requirements
DR spectrum: hot (synchronized replica, minutes), warm (configured infrastructure, hours), cold (empty facility, days/weeks), cloud (elastic, configurable), each with cost-benefit tradeoffs
Ransomware-resilient backup: immutable WORM storage, air-gapped offline copies, regular restore testing, backup encryption with separately managed keys, and monitoring for unauthorized modification

Testing progression: tabletop (quarterly, validates decisions), walkthrough (semi-annual, validates procedures), simulation (annual, validates coordination), full interruption (biennial, validates actual recovery)
Plan maintenance: annual review, trigger-based updates for material changes, version control, distribution management, training for all BC personnel, and integration with security IR
Crisis communication: predefined templates per stakeholder group, trained spokespersons, outage-resistant channels, and regulatory notification coordination
IR integration: coordinated activation for combined security and business events, unified command, shared awareness, and recovery sequencing preserving forensic evidence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting BIA: identifying critical processes, determining MTPD, setting RPO/RTO targets, selecting DR strategies, and calculating investment cost-benefit

review.value: BIA identifies critical processes, max tolerable downtime, dependencies, and financial impact. Every subsequent BC/DR decision flows from BIA: hot standby for highest criticality, cold site for lowest.

Tabletop validates decisions, walkthrough validates procedures, simulation validates coordination, full interruption validates actual recovery. Organizations doing only tabletops never discover non-functional procedures.
Modern ransomware targets backups. Traditional assumptions may be invalid. Immutable, air-gapped, regularly tested backups are essential when adversaries specifically target recovery capability.
Platform generating $50K/hour, 4-hour MTPD, 24-hour cold site RTO = $1.2M exposure. Hot standby at $200K/year reduces to $12.5K. With biennial events: $593K annual savings, 197% ROI.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of third-party risk management
Apply standards for third-party risk management
Evaluate challenges in third-party risk management
Design solutions for third-party risk management
readings: Primary text on Third-Party Risk Management
NIST/ISO guidance on Third-Party Risk Management
Case studies on Third-Party Risk Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Third-party risk is a critical governance challenge as organizations depend on vendors who process, store, or access sensitive data. Target (HVAC vendor), SolarWinds (software update), and MOVEit (file transfer) breaches demonstrate security is only as strong as the weakest vendor. Mature TPRM covers the full lifecycle: due diligence before engagement, contractual requirements during negotiation, ongoing monitoring during the relationship, and secure offboarding at termination. Risk tiering ensures assessment rigor matches actual risk: critical cloud providers need comprehensive assessment while office supply vendors need basic verification. SOC 2 Type II reports require critical evaluation: check scope covers your services, review exceptions, and identify complementary user entity controls you must implement. Supply chain risk extends to fourth and fifth parties through cascading dependencies that are difficult to monitor. Vendor concentration creates single points of failure requiring business continuity planning for critical vendor outages or breaches.
key_concepts: Risk tiering: categorizing by data access, system connectivity, criticality, and regulatory impact into Critical/High/Medium/Low tiers determining assessment scope, frequency, and contractual requirements
Due diligence: SIG/CAIQ questionnaires, SOC 2 Type II review, ISO 27001 verification, pen test results, financial stability, and on-site assessment for critical vendors
SOC 2 evaluation: scope vs consumed services, management assertions, trust criteria coverage, exception analysis, compensating controls, and complementary user entity controls identification

Ongoing monitoring: annual reassessment for critical/high vendors, continuous monitoring via security ratings services, trigger-based reassessment for changes or breaches, and vendor breach intelligence
Contractual requirements: minimum security standards, audit rights, breach notification with compressed timelines, data handling/return/deletion, sub-processor management, and termination rights
Supply chain risk: fourth/fifth party cascading risk, software SBOM requirements, dependency vulnerability monitoring, and the challenge of visibility beyond direct vendor relationships
Concentration risk: single points of failure from critical dependencies, geographic concentration in cloud regions, and BC implications of critical vendor failure or breach
Offboarding: data return or certified deletion, credential revocation, network disconnection, equipment retrieval, and contractual destruction requirement verification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating SOC 2 Type II report: assessing scope, reviewing exceptions, identifying complementary controls, and producing vendor risk assessment summary

review.value: Not every vendor needs the same assessment. Critical cloud provider: comprehensive review. Office supply vendor: basic verification. Proportional effort prevents both fatigue and gaps.
Check scope covers your services, review exceptions affecting reliance, identify your complementary controls. A clean report for services you do not use provides zero assurance.
Single cloud provider in single region: outage affects all dependent processes. Diversification reduces risk but increases complexity. Risk tolerance determines the balance.
Your vendor's sub-processors create risk you may not see. Contractual flow-down and vendor transparency are essential but difficult to enforce at scale through multiple layers.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of security architecture governance
Apply standards for security architecture governance
Evaluate challenges in security architecture governance
Design solutions for security architecture governance
readings: Primary text on Security Architecture Governance
NIST/ISO guidance on Security Architecture Governance
Case studies on Security Architecture Governance

lecture_notes.introduction: Security architecture governance ensures security requirements are embedded in system design from inception rather than bolted on after deployment when remediation costs are 10-100x higher. Architecture review evaluates proposed designs against standards, regulatory requirements, and threat models before implementation. Secure-by-design principles translate governance into patterns: defense in depth, least privilege, zero trust, separation of duties, and fail-secure defaults. Threat modeling using STRIDE systematically identifies threats per component, assesses risk, and drives control selection. Cloud security governance addresses the shared responsibility model ensuring the organization implements all controls for its service model responsibilities. Architecture decision records document why choices were made, preventing future inadvertent reversal. DevSecOps integration embeds review into CI/CD pipelines through automated scanning and IaC validation, enabling security at development speed without creating bottlenecks.
key_concepts: Review process: mandatory triggers (new systems, significant changes, new data types), review board composition, evaluation criteria, and approval workflow with conditional and unconditional paths
Secure-by-design: defense in depth, least privilege, zero trust, separation of duties, fail-secure defaults as mandatory architectural principles for all new system designs
STRIDE threat modeling: systematic evaluation of each component for Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege with documented mitigations
Cloud architecture: shared responsibility by service type (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), CSPM for configuration compliance, cloud-native security evaluation, multi-cloud consistent policy enforcement
Reference patterns: microservices security (service mesh, mutual TLS), data security (encryption hierarchy, key management), identity (SSO, federation, MFA, PAM), network (ZTNA, microsegmentation)
Decision records: documenting context, options, rationale, consequences, and requirement traceability for security architecture choices creating institutional memory
Technical debt: tracking legacy systems, deprecated protocols, unsupported software, prioritizing remediation by risk, and including in risk reporting for governance visibility
DevSecOps: security in CI/CD through automated scanning, IaC validation, architecture compliance checking, enabling security governance at development speed
lecture_notes.demonstration: STRIDE threat model for cloud microservices: decomposing system, identifying threats per component, assessing risk, selecting controls, and presenting to architecture review board

review.value: Design-phase flaw identification costs 10-100x less than production remediation. Missing encryption in design review: design change. Same discovery after deployment: data layer re-engineering.
Ensures comprehensive coverage by evaluating each component for all six threat categories. Prevents ad-hoc identification that misses entire categories.

Cloud provider secures infrastructure; customer secures what they build. In IaaS: customer owns OS patching, network config. In SaaS: customer owns access management. Misunderstanding causes breaches.
Document why choices were made to prevent future reversal: 'We encrypt all fields because performance is acceptable and it eliminates classification error risk.'

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of identity and access management governance
Apply standards for identity and access management governance
Evaluate challenges in identity and access management governance
Design solutions for identity and access management governance
readings: Primary text on Identity and Access Management Governance
NIST/ISO guidance on Identity and Access Management Governance
Case studies on Identity and Access Management Governance

lecture_notes.introduction: IAM governance is the most impactful security domain because identity underlies every security decision. IAM failures feature in virtually every major breach: compromised credentials for initial access, excessive permissions enabling lateral movement, orphaned accounts as persistent backdoors. Effective programs address the full lifecycle: automated provisioning from HR, role-based assignment from defined catalogs, modification workflows for role changes, and same-day deprovisioning on termination. Privileged access management eliminates standing admin access through just-in-time elevation with approval, time limits, session recording, and automatic revocation. Access reviews detect permission creep where employees accumulate access over years through role changes without revocation. Non-human identities (service accounts, API keys) often outnumber human identities 10:1, frequently have excessive permissions, and are excluded from reviews. Zero trust principles require continuous verification, context-aware decisions, least privilege with JIT elevation, and assumed breach posture.
key_concepts: Lifecycle: automated HR-triggered provisioning, role-based assignment from catalogs, modification workflows for changes/transfers, same-day termination deprovisioning ensuring immediate access revocation
PAM: eliminate standing admin access, JIT elevation with approval and time limits (1-4 hours), session recording for all privileged operations, vault-based credential management for service accounts
Access review: quarterly for sensitive access, semi-annual for all, manager certification of role appropriateness, automated detection of unused access, escalation for overdue reviews
RBAC design: role engineering mapping functions to permissions, role catalog, role mining from existing patterns, SoD enforcement through mutually exclusive roles preventing fraud and error
Non-human identities: service account inventory and ownership, API key lifecycle with rotation, machine identity certificates, inclusion in reviews with accountable owners for each identity
Cloud identity: centralized IdP for SSO, SCIM provisioning to SaaS, conditional access incorporating device compliance and risk signals, cross-platform visibility and governance
Analytics: anomalous access pattern detection, excessive permission identification via peer comparison, dormant account flagging, impossible travel detection for compromised credentials
Zero trust: continuous verification per request, context-aware decisions with risk signals, least privilege with JIT, assumed breach requiring defense-in-depth around identity infrastructure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing PAM program: inventorying privileged accounts, defining JIT workflows, configuring session recording, implementing vault-based management, and creating governance dashboard

review.value: Five-year employees may have access from three prior roles. Quarterly reviews detect this creep and drive revocation before exploitation.
Standing admin = immediate full access upon compromise. JIT = no privileges until approved, time-limited elevation auto-revokes. Exposure window shrinks from permanent to 1-4 hours.
Service accounts and API keys outnumber humans 10:1, have excessive permissions, skip reviews, and never rotate credentials. Massive ungoverned attack surface.
No one initiates and approves the same transaction. RBAC enforces through mutually exclusive roles: developer cannot deploy to production; payment initiator cannot approve.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of security operations governance
Apply standards for security operations governance
Evaluate challenges in security operations governance
Design solutions for security operations governance
readings: Primary text on Security Operations Governance
NIST/ISO guidance on Security Operations Governance
Case studies on Security Operations Governance

lecture_notes.introduction: SecOps governance ensures detection, response, and remediation capabilities operate effectively at scale. SOC governance covers staffing models, skill development, escalation procedures, playbook maintenance, and quality metrics distinguishing alert processing from actual threat detection. Vulnerability management governance defines risk-based prioritization (CVSS + EPSS + asset criticality + exploit availability), SLAs by severity tier, exception processes, and metrics tracking backlog trends. Patch management bridges security urgency with operations stability through defined windows, emergency procedures, and rollback plans. Change management security ensures modifications follow approved processes preventing unauthorized changes that introduce vulnerabilities. Each domain requires policy, standards, metrics, reporting, and improvement processes at the maturity level appropriate for the organization's risk profile.
key_concepts: SOC governance: model selection (in-house/MSSP/hybrid), tier structure with escalation, skill development, playbook library maintenance, quality assurance through detection testing and purple team exercises
SOC metrics: alert volume/triage rate, MTTD/MTTR, false positive rate, ATT&CK detection coverage, analyst utilization and burnout indicators, purple team exercise results validating capability

Vulnerability management: risk-based prioritization (CVSS+EPSS+KEV+asset criticality), SLAs by tier (Critical/Internet 48h, Critical/Internal 7d, High 30d, Medium 90d), exception management, velocity metrics
Patch management: regular cycles with testing, emergency procedures for actively exploited vulnerabilities, rollback plans, compliance metrics, and change management coordination for stability

Change management security: review requirements for changes, pre-implementation testing, emergency change procedures with post-implementation review, unauthorized change detection, change-incident correlation
Incident classification: severity criteria with SLAs, escalation triggers and communication requirements, executive notification thresholds, and regulatory reporting trigger assessment per incident
Operational resilience: SOC redundancy and failover, on-call procedures, geographic distribution for coverage, knowledge management preventing single points of failure, succession planning
Continuous improvement: post-incident reviews, regular effectiveness assessment through exercises, metrics-driven development, and benchmark comparison against operational maturity frameworks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing VM governance program: risk-based prioritization methodology, SLA targets, exception management, metrics dashboard, and monthly governance review process

review.value: CVSS alone is insufficient. Combine with EPSS, CISA KEV, asset exposure, and business criticality. True risk ranking focuses remediation where it matters most.
Processing alerts and closing them is not effective. Detection rate for real attacks, FP rate, and escalation speed matter. Purple teams provide ground truth.
Security wants immediate patching; operations wants stability. Governance resolves through defined windows, risk-based acceleration for exploited vulnerabilities, and emergency procedures.
Significant percentage of incidents caused by changes: misconfigs, insecure defaults, vulnerable components. Correlating incidents with change timeline identifies root causes.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of data governance and classification
Apply standards for data governance and classification
Evaluate challenges in data governance and classification
Design solutions for data governance and classification
readings: Primary text on Data Governance and Classification
NIST/ISO guidance on Data Governance and Classification
Case studies on Data Governance and Classification

lecture_notes.introduction: Data governance and classification form the foundation for all data protection controls. Without knowing what data you have, where it is, how sensitive it is, and who owns it, rational security investment is impossible. Classification defines sensitivity tiers (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) with specific control requirements per tier for storage, transmission, access, sharing, and disposal. When effective, classification drives automated controls: Restricted data is automatically encrypted with strongest algorithms, access restricted to approved roles with MFA, DLP prevents unauthorized sharing, and retention policies ensure timely disposal. Governance assigns accountability through data owners (business leaders for classification and access decisions), data stewards (operational quality and compliance management), and data custodians (IT implementing technical controls). Automated discovery tools scan repositories using pattern matching to identify sensitive data, creating comprehensive inventories. Data loss prevention deploys across endpoints, network, and cloud to enforce classification-driven controls.
key_concepts: Classification tiers: Public (unrestricted), Internal (organization-only), Confidential (regulated data requiring encryption and access control), Restricted (highest sensitivity requiring strongest controls and monitoring)
Classification-driven controls: each tier maps to encryption, access control, DLP, retention, and disposal requirements creating graduated protection proportional to sensitivity
Governance roles: owner (business leader for classification/access), steward (operational quality/compliance), custodian (IT technical control implementation) with clear accountability
Automated discovery: scanning databases, file shares, cloud storage for sensitive patterns (card numbers, SSNs, health records), creating inventory with classification, location, and owner
DLP: endpoint (removable media, personal cloud), network (email, web uploads), cloud (storage scanning), API (SaaS data flows), configured by classification tier for graduated enforcement
Retention governance: regulatory requirements (HIPAA 6yr, SOX 7yr, GDPR purpose limitation), legal hold, automated enforcement, and secure disposal verification
Data sovereignty: regulatory residency requirements, cloud region selection, DR architecture impact, and cross-border transfer compliance verification
Transfer mechanisms: GDPR SCCs, EU-US DPF, BCRs, adequacy decisions, and supplementary measures for Schrems II transfer assessment requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing classification program: defining tiers with controls, creating decision tree, implementing discovery, configuring DLP by tier, and building governance dashboard

review.value: Classification drives every data control. Without it, you cannot determine what to encrypt, who should access, how long to retain, or when to delete. First step: every protection decision depends on sensitivity.
Manual classification fails at scale. Automated tools scan using pattern matching and ML to identify and classify sensitive data, ensuring comprehensive coverage across all repositories.
Deploy monitoring-only first to understand flows and tune rules. Then progressively enable blocking for clear violations while monitoring borderline cases requiring human review.
GDPR deletion must reach all systems: production, backups, analytics, warehouses, third-party processors. Without governance knowing where data resides, verifying erasure is impossible.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of regulatory audit management
Apply standards for regulatory audit management
Evaluate challenges in regulatory audit management
Design solutions for regulatory audit management
readings: Primary text on Regulatory Audit Management
NIST/ISO guidance on Regulatory Audit Management
Case studies on Regulatory Audit Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Regulatory audits verify that controls operate as claimed. Whether PCI-DSS QSA, HIPAA OCR, ISO 27001 certification, or SOC 2 examination, the process involves independent evaluation of control design effectiveness and operating effectiveness over a defined period. Audit management encompasses preparation (maintaining continuous evidence), execution (coordinating requests, managing access, ensuring SME availability), and follow-through (root cause analysis, remediation, verification, prevention). Organizations viewing audits as collaborative assessments achieve better outcomes than those treating them as adversarial encounters. Building internal audit capability that identifies issues before external auditors is the most effective strategy. Common findings include insufficient evidence of operation, inconsistent implementation across units, policy-practice gaps, inadequate access reviews, and incomplete remediation tracking. Root cause discipline ensures findings are addressed systemically rather than symptomatically, preventing recurrence.
key_concepts: Preparation: continuous evidence maintenance, 90-day readiness assessment, evidence pre-staging by domain, stakeholder briefing, designated liaisons for each assessment area
Evidence presentation: organization by control objective, automated collection from sources, evidence demonstrating consistent operation over audit period, integrity through access controls
Auditor relationships: transparency, prompt information provision, proactive disclosure of known issues with remediation plans, professional communication maintaining constructive partnership
Finding management: root cause analysis of systemic failure, remediation with milestones and owners, management approval, verification testing, and preventive measures for similar controls
Internal audit: independent function reporting to board audit committee, risk-based plan, IIA/ISACA methodology, and coordination with external auditors reducing duplication
PCI-DSS: scope validation before assessment, compensating control documentation, sampling for large populations, clear remediation requirements for missing controls
ISO 27001: Stage 1 documentation review, Stage 2 on-site audit, annual surveillance, triennial recertification, with major and minor nonconformity classification
Common findings: insufficient operation evidence, inconsistent implementation, policy-practice gaps, inadequate access reviews, incomplete remediation tracking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Preparing for simulated ISO 27001 Stage 2: organizing Annex A control evidence, mock interviews, addressing findings with root cause analysis, and presenting readiness assessment

review.value: Organizations maintaining continuous evidence need days of preparation. Those scrambling need weeks and risk gaps. Automated collection is the foundation.
Symptomatic fixes ensure recurrence. Root cause analysis identifies systemic failures in design, implementation, or monitoring. Only root cause remediation prevents repeated findings.
Finds issues before external auditors, enabling advance remediation. Independent, reporting to board audit committee, coordinating with external auditors.
PCI scope determines assessment extent. Segmentation, tokenization, and P2PE reduce scope dramatically. Validate at start to prevent costly mid-audit expansion.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of cybersecurity program maturity and benchmarking
Apply standards for cybersecurity program maturity and benchmarking
Evaluate challenges in cybersecurity program maturity and benchmarking
Design solutions for cybersecurity program maturity and benchmarking
readings: Primary text on Cybersecurity Program Maturity and Benchmarking
NIST/ISO guidance on Cybersecurity Program Maturity and Benchmarking
Case studies on Cybersecurity Program Maturity and Benchmarking

lecture_notes.introduction: Maturity assessment evaluates how consistently and effectively security processes operate, going beyond compliance checkbox verification. An organization may have vulnerability management policy (compliance met) but execute scanning sporadically with no prioritization (Level 1 maturity). Another with the same policy has automated scanning, risk-based prioritization, SLA tracking, exceptions, and metrics-driven improvement (Level 4). Both satisfy compliance but deliver enormously different risk reduction. NIST CSF Implementation Tiers, C2M2, and CMMI provide structured scales. Benchmarking compares against industry peers, providing context: a score of 2.5/5 may be excellent for a startup but concerning for a major bank. Improvement roadmaps define target maturity per domain, create phased milestones, and estimate resources. Business cases quantify risk reduction from improvements using FAIR, calculate efficiency gains, and present ROI. Maturity progression takes years, not months: plan 6-12 months per level with quick wins front-loaded to demonstrate value.
key_concepts: NIST CSF Tiers: Partial (ad hoc), Risk-Informed (approved but not universal), Repeatable (formally approved, consistent), Adaptive (actively adapting based on lessons and prediction)
C2M2: Not Performed, Initiated, Performed, Managed, with management practice indicators assessing documentation, responsibility, training, measurement, and improvement
Assessment methodology: self-assessment questionnaire, evidence review, gap analysis between current and target, and prioritized improvement plan addressing highest-impact gaps first
Benchmarking: comparing against similar-size/industry/risk organizations, identifying below-average domains, using benchmark data to prioritize and justify investment decisions
Roadmap development: target maturity per domain from risk appetite, 1/3/5-year milestones, resource estimates, measurable success criteria, and dependency identification
Business case: FAIR risk reduction quantification, efficiency gains from automation, compliance cost avoidance, and total ROI presentation for board investment approval
Maturity vs compliance: compliance asks 'Do you have it?' Maturity asks 'How well does it work?' Compliant with immature processes or mature exceeding compliance minimums are very different postures
Program tracking: quarterly maturity scoring against milestones, investment vs planned risk reduction, and annual roadmap recalibration based on lessons and changing landscape
lecture_notes.demonstration: NIST CSF maturity assessment: evaluating each function, comparing against targets, creating gap analysis, and building three-year improvement business case

review.value: Compliance verifies existence. Maturity verifies effectiveness. PCI-compliant with Level 1 maturity: controls exist but execute manually and inconsistently. Level 3: documented, consistent, measured, improving.
A 2.5/5 score needs context. 25th percentile for financial services clearly indicates underinvestment. Peer comparison makes abstract scores actionable for budget conversations.
Level 1 to 4 takes years. Each level builds on the previous. Plan 6-12 months per level. Quick wins front-loaded to demonstrate value and maintain stakeholder support.
Every investment as risk reduction: '$1.2M VM automation reduces ransomware exposure from $8M to $2M: 5x ROI year one.' Financial framing drives board approval.

learning_objectives: Analyze principles of capstone: integrated security governance program
Apply standards for capstone: integrated security governance program
Evaluate challenges in capstone: integrated security governance program
Design solutions for capstone: integrated security governance program
readings: Primary text on Capstone: Integrated Security Governance Program
NIST/ISO guidance on Capstone: Integrated Security Governance Program
Case studies on Capstone: Integrated Security Governance Program

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective governance integrates all domains into a coherent framework: individual components provide limited value in isolation, but integration creates multiplier effects where risk findings drive policy updates, incident lessons improve compliance monitoring, and metrics inform board reporting. The framework must be adaptive: threat landscape evolves continuously, regulations change rapidly, and business conditions transform through M&A, digital transformation, and market expansion. Adaptive mechanisms include regulatory intelligence for emerging requirements, threat intelligence informing risk updates, technology scanning for new capabilities, and continuous improvement from all organizational experiences. Emerging challenges include AI governance for autonomous security tools, supply chain governance for exponentially growing dependencies, geopolitical risk navigation, and quantum cryptographic preparedness. The most effective CISOs position governance as enabling business innovation: mature governance accelerates cloud adoption and digital transformation by providing clear frameworks and automated compliance. Sustainable programs embed governance into processes, automate controls, and distribute accountability rather than depending on individual champions.
key_concepts: Integrated architecture: connecting framework, policies, compliance, risk, operations governance, and reporting into a coherent system with feedback loops where each component strengthens others
Adaptive mechanisms: regulatory intelligence monitoring, threat intelligence informing risk, technology scanning for capabilities, continuous improvement incorporating all lessons learned
AI governance: policies for AI in security operations, accuracy and bias governance, human oversight requirements, managing adversarial AI risks, and vendor AI tool accountability
Supply chain evolution: comprehensive security including open source dependencies, SBOM governance, build pipeline integrity, and visibility beyond direct vendor relationships
Geopolitical risk: cybersecurity implications of international tensions, sanctions compliance, data sovereignty planning, and technology dependency diversification
Quantum preparedness: crypto-agility roadmap, PQC transition planning, cryptographic dependency inventory, and quantum risk in long-term risk assessments
Security as enabler: mature governance accelerates adoption: 'Pre-approved patterns and automated compliance reduce cloud onboarding from months to weeks.' Framing gains executive support.
Sustainability: embedding governance in processes, automating controls, distributing accountability, and maintaining documentation enabling continuity through leadership transitions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Presenting integrated governance program: synthesizing all domains into unified proposal with board presentation format

review.value: Individual components limited alone. Integration creates multiplier effects: risk drives policy, incidents improve compliance, metrics inform reporting. Whole exceeds sum of parts.
Framework for today's threats is obsolete in 2-3 years. Adaptive mechanisms ensure evolution with environment rather than periodic complete redesign.
Most effective CISOs position governance as enabling objectives: 'Mature program allows new cloud services in 2 weeks instead of 3 months.' This framing gains executive support.
Programs dependent on a single champion collapse on departure. Embed in processes, automate controls, distribute accountability. Enable continuity through any transition.

questions.question: A company achieved ISO 27001 certification but suffered a breach from an 8-month unpatched vulnerability. Explain how this is possible and identify governance failures.
questions.answer: ISO 27001 certifies the ISMS exists, not that every control works perfectly always. Failures: (1) VM process (A.8.8) defined but not monitored. (2) Continuous monitoring (9.1) inadequate. (3) Management review (9.3) did not surface gap. (4) Internal audit (9.2) missed or unremediated. Certification is point-in-time; governance ensures continuous compliance.

questions.question: Draft a policy statement for Data at Rest Encryption and write the corresponding standard with algorithms, key lengths, and key management.
questions.answer: Policy: 'All Confidential/Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using approved methods. Keys managed through approved KMS.' Standard: 'AES-256-GCM, FIPS 140-2 CSPRNG, HSM/approved cloud KMS with FIPS 140-2 Level 3, annual DEK rotation, 3-year KEK rotation, dual-person HSM admin. BitLocker/LUKS for endpoints, TDE for databases, CMK for cloud.'

questions.question: Organization subject to HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and SOC 2. Describe how to build a unified control framework avoiding duplication.
questions.answer: Select NIST CSF as primary. Map each control to all three requirements. Shared controls (MFA, encryption, logging) implemented once. Framework-specific (HIPAA BAA, PCI ASV scans, SOC 2 availability) as additions. Unified matrix with GRC platform for automated evidence across all three from single control set.

questions.question: Board briefing: 2,400 critical vulns, 12-day MTTD, 18% phishing rate, 3 incidents. Translate to business risk with investment recommendations.
questions.answer: 'Exposure elevated: (1) 2,400 criticals = ~$8.5M risk. $500K patching reduces to $1.2M. (2) 12-day MTTD vs 7-day median. $300K EDR/SIEM reduces to 4 days. (3) 18% phishing = 3x average. $150K awareness targets 5%. Total: $950K for 70% risk reduction with measurable ROI.'

questions.question: BIA: e-commerce, MTPD 4h, $50K/hour. DR: 24h cold site. Calculate gap, recommend upgrade, cost-benefit.
questions.answer: Gap: MTPD=4h, RTO=24h, 20h gap. Exposure: $1.2M/incident. Hot standby: RTO 15min, $200K/yr. Biennial events: current ALE=$600K, new=$6.25K. Savings: $593.75K/yr. ROI: 197%.

questions.question: Three phishing campaigns: C1 22%click/3%report, C2 15%/12%, C3 11%/8%. Analyze trends, identify C3 concern.
questions.answer: Positive: click declining. Report rate improved from baseline. C3 concern: report rate dropped to 8% despite continued click improvement. Simulation fatigue: employees recognize and delete without reporting. Recommendations: vary templates, reinforce reporting, add rewards, track real phishing reporting separately.

questions.question: Vendor SOC 2 notes 45-day unencrypted database. Evaluate and determine actions.
questions.answer: Evaluate: (1) Was your data in affected database? (2) 45 days is significant. (3) Root cause and remediation quality. Actions: (1) Request data impact details. (2) Assess if reportable breach. (3) Increase risk rating. (4) Require monitoring improvements. (5) Consider audit rights.

questions.question: STRIDE identifies Elevation of Privilege via SQL injection. What architectural controls?
questions.answer: (1) Parameterized queries (primary defense). (2) Least privilege DB account. (3) WAF with SQLi detection. (4) SAST/DAST in CI/CD. (5) Runtime protection or DB activity monitoring. (6) Output encoding.

questions.question: Access review finds finance manager has 2-year-old developer production DB access. Governance failures?
questions.answer: Failures: (1) Mover process did not revoke prior role access. (2) Reviews missed excessive access. (3) No SoD enforcement between finance/developer. Improvements: (1) Automated role-change from HR. (2) Review training for certifiers. (3) Automated outlier detection. (4) SoD rules.

questions.question: 5,000 vulns, 200/week capacity. Critical 7-day SLA: 300. High 30-day: 800. Does capacity meet SLAs?
questions.answer: Critical: 300 in 7 days = 43/day, capacity 40/day. Shortfall. High: after Criticals (~8 days), 22 days remain. 620 capacity vs 800 needed. Shortfall 180. Governance: escalate, risk-prioritize within Critical using EPSS, request automation budget, document acceptance with compensating controls, present quantified risk.

questions.question: DLP blocks VP Sales emailing customer proposal with Confidential pricing. VP demands exception. Handle?
questions.answer: (1) Validate classification. (2) Evaluate: proposals are legitimate business, DLP may be overbroad. (3) Alternatives: secure file sharing. (4) Tune DLP for customer domains. (5) If exception: formal request with conditions. (6) Update policy if common use case.

questions.question: ISO 27001 surveillance: no formal Clause 9.3 management review in 12 months. Impact?
questions.answer: Major nonconformity. Clause 9.3 requires formal reviews with specific inputs/outputs. Informal updates insufficient. May suspend certification. Remediate: immediate formal review, establish schedule, create agenda template, document outputs, root cause: implement accountability.

questions.question: Maturity: VM Level 1, Access Control Level 2, IR Level 3. Design 12-month plan.
questions.answer: VM at L1 = highest risk. VM (L1->L3): Months 1-3: automated scanning, SLAs, tracking. Months 4-6: risk prioritization, ticketing, exceptions. Months 7-12: continuous monitoring, trends, documentation. Access Control (L2->L3): Months 3-6: RBAC, formalize reviews. Months 7-12: automation, JIT PAM. IR (maintain L3): quarterly exercises, automated playbooks.

questions.question: Board asks: 'Are we spending enough?' Answer with quantitative evidence.
questions.answer: (1) FAIR top 10 risks with annualized exposure. (2) Map spending to risk reduction. (3) Calculate marginal reduction per additional dollar. (4) Benchmark spending vs peers. (5) Present: 'Current $5M reduces $45M to $12M (73%). Additional $1.5M reduces to $5M (89%). At 30th percentile; investment brings to 55th with 3.6x ROI.'

questions.question: How must governance adapt for AI security tools making autonomous blocking decisions?
questions.answer: (1) Define acceptable FP/FN rates with thresholds for human review. (2) Policy for automated vs human-confirmed decisions. (3) Bias monitoring. (4) Explainability requirements. (5) Override procedures. (6) Vendor accuracy SLAs. (7) Track AI errors as metrics.

major_assignments.title: Security Governance Program Design
major_assignments.description: Design complete governance program for mid-size healthcare company with PHI and payment data.
objectives: Develop five core policies with standards
Design unified compliance for HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC 2
Create executive dashboard with board report

criteria: Develop five core policies with standards
Design unified compliance for HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC 2
Create executive dashboard with board report

major_assignments.title: Third-Party Risk Management Program
major_assignments.description: Design TPRM program for 200 vendors including 15 critical cloud providers.

major_assignments.title: Enterprise Maturity Assessment and Roadmap
major_assignments.description: Comprehensive maturity assessment, peer benchmarking, three-year roadmap, and board business case.

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes on governance, compliance, risk, operations, and maturity

lab_assignments.description: Policy drafting, compliance mapping, risk quantification, and audit exercises

major_assignments.description: Three assignments: governance design, TPRM, and maturity assessment

midterm_examination.description: Midterm on governance frameworks, policies, compliance, and risk

final_project.description: Final project on comprehensive governance competency

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Group discussions encouraged. All written deliverables must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Using templates as starting points is acceptable but content must demonstrate genuine understanding.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to required tools and platforms will be provided. Students must have a laptop meeting minimum specifications.
